license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.notice: Event descriptions are paraphrased summaries written for this dataset. Source links point to authoritative references.

entries.title: A Kerberos authentication ticket (TGT) was requested
entries.summary: A Kerberos TGT was requested from the Key Distribution Center (KDC).
entries.details: DC-only event generated whenever a Kerberos Authentication Service Request (AS-REQ) is processed by the KDC. Key fields: TargetUserName (the account requesting the ticket), TicketEncryptionType (0x12=AES256, 0x11=AES128, 0x17=RC4, 0x18=DES — RC4 or DES indicates a downgrade), IpAddress (the requesting host), TicketOptions (bitmask of requested ticket flags), ResultCode (0x0=success, 0x6=username does not exist, 0x12=account disabled/locked/expired, 0x18=wrong password). AS-REP roasting: when an account has pre-authentication disabled (DONT_REQ_PREAUTH), an attacker can request a TGT without knowing the password and receive an encrypted blob to crack offline — these appear as successful 4768 events without a preceding pre-auth exchange, and no corresponding 4771 failure will be generated. Golden Ticket abuse: forged TGTs may appear with unusual TicketOptions or encryption types inconsistent with domain policy. IpAddresses that are not domain-joined machines requesting TGTs for privileged accounts are anomalous. This event also fires as AuditFailure for failed TGT requests.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Steal or Forge Kerberos Tickets: Golden Ticket
Steal or Forge Kerberos Tickets: AS-REP Roasting
Use Alternate Authentication Material: Pass the Ticket

investigationPivots: TicketEncryptionType 0x17 (RC4-HMAC) when the environment enforces AES indicates a downgrade attack or legacy client
Successful TGT for an account that has pre-auth disabled (check msDS-SupportedEncryptionTypes or DONT_REQ_PREAUTH flag) without a corresponding 4771 failure is an AS-REP roasting indicator
IpAddress that is not domain-joined, a DC, or a known privileged admin workstation requesting TGTs for sensitive accounts is anomalous
TicketOptions 0x40810010 (forwardable+renewable+canonicalize) is normal; unusual combinations may indicate Golden Ticket usage
High volume of 4768 events for the same TargetUserName from many different IpAddresses may indicate credential stuffing or distributed brute force
commonFalsePositives: Normal Kerberos authentication for all domain users — extremely high volume on DCs
Legacy systems genuinely requiring RC4 (TicketEncryptionType 0x17) due to application compatibility
Kerberos authentication from non-domain-joined Linux/Mac machines that support Kerberos

keyFields.name: TargetUserName
keyFields.description: Account requesting the TGT; look for service accounts or computer accounts in unusual requests

keyFields.name: TicketEncryptionType
keyFields.description: Encryption type of the issued ticket; 0x17 (RC4-HMAC) is a Kerberoasting/downgrade indicator

keyFields.name: PreAuthType
keyFields.description: Pre-authentication type; 0 means pre-auth not required (AS-REP roasting target)

keyFields.name: IpAddress
keyFields.description: Source IP of the AS-REQ; non-DC IPs requesting TGTs for privileged accounts are suspicious

keyFields.name: TicketOptions
keyFields.description: Bitmask of requested ticket flags; unusual options may indicate Golden Ticket use

detectionRules.title: AS-REP Roasting — Pre-Auth Not Required
detectionRules.rule:
SecurityEvent
| where EventID == 4768
| where PreAuthType == "0"
| where TargetUserName !endswith "$"
| summarize count() by TargetUserName, IpAddress, bin(TimeGenerated, 1h)
| where count_ > 5
detectionRules.notes: PreAuthType 0 means Kerberos pre-authentication is disabled — attacker can request AS-REP and crack offline. Tune threshold per environment.

detectionRules.title: AS-REP Roasting Detection
detectionRules.rule:
index=wineventlog EventCode=4768 Pre_Authentication_Type=0 NOT Account_Name="*$"
| stats count by Account_Name, Client_Address
| where count > 5
detectionRules.notes: Detects AS-REP roasting attempts against accounts with pre-auth disabled.

detectionRules.title: Kerberos AS-REP Roasting and RC4 Downgrade
detectionRules.rule:
title: Kerberos AS-REP Roasting and RC4 Downgrade
status: experimental
logsource:
    product: windows
    service: security
detection:
    selection:
        EventID: 4768
    filter_preauth:
        PreAuthType: '0'
    filter_rc4:
        TicketEncryptionType:
            - '0x17'
            - '0x18'
    condition: selection and (filter_preauth or filter_rc4)
falsepositives:
    - Legacy systems requiring RC4
    - Accounts with pre-authentication intentionally disabled
level: medium
detectionRules.notes: Match on either pre-auth disabled or RC4 encryption type.

entries.title: A Kerberos service ticket was requested
entries.summary: A Kerberos TGS service ticket was requested from the KDC.
entries.details: DC-only event generated whenever a Kerberos Ticket Granting Service Request (TGS-REQ) is processed by the KDC. Key fields: ServiceName (the SPN being requested — e.g., MSSQLSvc/server.domain.com), TicketEncryptionType (0x17=RC4 is the primary Kerberoasting indicator), IpAddress (the requesting host), TicketOptions (bitmask), ResultCode. Kerberoasting: an attacker requests TGS tickets for SPN-bearing service accounts using RC4 encryption (0x17), receives an encrypted blob hashed with the service account password, and cracks it offline. Detection indicators include bulk 4769 events for multiple SPNs from one source, or a single 4769 with RC4 for a high-privilege SPN. Silver Ticket: forged service tickets do not hit the KDC at all and therefore do not generate 4769 events — the absence of a 4769 for a targeted service while that service is being accessed is itself an indicator. RODC key compromise may be indicated by 4769 events for sensitive accounts originating from RODCs.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Steal or Forge Kerberos Tickets: Kerberoasting
Steal or Forge Kerberos Tickets: Silver Ticket
Use Alternate Authentication Material: Pass the Ticket

investigationPivots: TicketEncryptionType 0x17 (RC4) for a ServiceName associated with a high-privilege service account (Domain Admin service account, SQL SA) is the primary Kerberoasting indicator
Multiple 4769 events in rapid succession from one IpAddress targeting different SPNs indicates automated Kerberoasting (Rubeus, Impacket GetUserSPNs)
ServiceName for krbtgt with RC4 encryption is a Golden Ticket usage indicator
4769 with failure code 0x20 (expired ticket) is routine and not indicative of attack
IpAddress cross-referenced with EID 4768 for the same source — legitimate users request TGT first, then TGS; missing TGT may indicate pass-the-ticket
commonFalsePositives: High-volume TGS requests are normal for any authenticated user accessing network services
RC4 TGS requests from legacy applications or non-Windows clients that do not support AES
Service accounts with SPNs legitimately authenticating between services

keyFields.name: ServiceName
keyFields.description: SPN of the service ticket requested; Kerberoasting targets service accounts with SPNs

keyFields.name: TicketEncryptionType
keyFields.description: 0x17 (RC4-HMAC) TGS requests for SPN accounts are the core Kerberoasting indicator

keyFields.name: IpAddress
keyFields.description: Source IP; bulk TGS requests from a single IP suggest automated Kerberoasting

keyFields.name: Status
keyFields.description: Result code; 0x0 = success, non-zero = failure with reason

keyFields.name: TicketOptions
keyFields.description: Requested ticket flags; Silver Ticket indicators include forwardable flag mismatches

detectionRules.title: Kerberoasting — RC4 TGS Requests
detectionRules.rule:
SecurityEvent
| where EventID == 4769
| where TicketEncryptionType == "0x17"
| where ServiceName !endswith "$"
| where Status == "0x0"
| summarize RequestCount = count(), Services = make_set(ServiceName) by IpAddress, Account, bin(TimeGenerated, 10m)
| where RequestCount > 3
detectionRules.notes: RC4 TGS requests for non-machine accounts. Threshold of 3+ in 10 min catches automated tools like Rubeus and Impacket GetUserSPNs.

detectionRules.title: Kerberoasting RC4 Service Ticket Requests
detectionRules.rule:
index=wineventlog EventCode=4769 Ticket_Encryption_Type=0x17 NOT Service_Name="*$"
| stats count as RequestCount values(Service_Name) as Services by Client_Address, Account_Name
| where RequestCount > 3
detectionRules.notes: High volume RC4 TGS requests from a single source suggest Kerberoasting.

detectionRules.title: Kerberoasting Service Ticket Request with RC4
detectionRules.rule:
title: Kerberoasting Service Ticket Request with RC4
status: experimental
logsource:
    product: windows
    service: security
detection:
    selection:
        EventID: 4769
        TicketEncryptionType:
            - '0x17'
            - '0x18'
        Status: '0x0'
    filter_machine:
        ServiceName|endswith: '$'
    condition: selection and not filter_machine
falsepositives:
    - Legacy applications requiring RC4 Kerberos encryption
level: medium
detectionRules.notes: Filter machine account TGS (ending in $) to reduce noise.

entries.title: A Kerberos service ticket was renewed
entries.summary: A Kerberos service ticket was renewed by the KDC.
entries.details: DC-only event generated when a Kerberos service ticket is renewed before it expires. Key fields: ServiceName (the SPN for the renewed ticket), TicketEncryptionType, IpAddress. Ticket renewals are expected for long-running sessions such as RDP sessions, database connections, and print server connections. Adversaries using pass-the-ticket or overpass-the-hash may trigger anomalous renewals — a ticket being renewed from a different IpAddress than the original TGS request (EID 4769), or renewal of a high-privilege service ticket long after the associated logon session should have ended, are investigation pivots. Renewal of a krbtgt ticket, which should not normally occur, may indicate Golden Ticket abuse with an extended MaxRenewAge setting; cross-reference EID 4713 for Kerberos policy changes.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Use Alternate Authentication Material: Pass the Ticket
Steal or Forge Kerberos Tickets: Golden Ticket

investigationPivots: Renewal IpAddress differing from the original 4769 TGS request IpAddress may indicate ticket theft and use from a different host
Renewal of tickets for highly privileged service accounts (Domain Admins, Exchange, backup services) outside business hours is anomalous
Renewal of krbtgt service tickets should not occur in normal operations — investigate immediately
Correlate ticket lifetime against domain Kerberos policy (EID 4713) — unusually long renewal windows may have been set by an attacker to extend Golden Ticket validity
commonFalsePositives: Long-running legitimate sessions (RDP, database, file server connections) that renew tickets before expiry
Service accounts with persistent connections (scheduled jobs, monitoring agents) renewing tickets automatically

keyFields.name: ServiceName
keyFields.description: Service ticket being renewed; watch for unusual service accounts

keyFields.name: TicketEncryptionType
keyFields.description: Encryption type; RC4 renewal may indicate pass-the-ticket activity

keyFields.name: TargetUserName
keyFields.description: Account whose ticket is being renewed

keyFields.name: TicketOptions
keyFields.description: Renewed ticket flags; renewable flag must be set; mismatches can indicate manipulation

entries.title: Kerberos pre-authentication failed
entries.summary: A Kerberos pre-authentication request failed at the KDC.
entries.details: DC-only event generated when the KDC fails to issue a TGT during pre-authentication. Key fields: TargetUserName (the account for which pre-auth failed), IpAddress (the client), FailureCode. Important failure codes: 0x17 (password expired), 0x18 (wrong password — brute force or spray indicator), 0x10 (certificate or smart card error), 0x12 (account disabled, locked, or expired), 0x25 (clock skew too large — Kerberos requires clocks within 5 minutes), 0x6 (client not found — username enumeration indicator). Important caveat: if an account has pre-authentication disabled (DONT_REQ_PREAUTH), authentication failures are NOT captured by this event — they appear as successes in EID 4768 and require offline cracking (AS-REP roasting). This event only fires for Kerberos authentication failures, not NTLM; failed NTLM authentications are recorded in EID 4625.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Steal or Forge Kerberos Tickets: AS-REP Roasting
Brute Force: Password Spraying
Brute Force: Password Guessing

investigationPivots: FailureCode 0x18 (wrong password) across many different TargetUserName values from one IpAddress in a short window indicates a password spray attack
FailureCode 0x18 repeatedly against a single TargetUserName from one or few IpAddresses indicates brute force
FailureCode 0x6 (client not found) in volume from one source indicates username enumeration
FailureCode 0x12 immediately after high-volume 0x18 failures for the same account indicates account lockout confirming brute force
IpAddress that is external, from an unusual network segment, or a known-bad IP range elevates all failure codes to critical
commonFalsePositives: Users with genuinely expired or forgotten passwords generating isolated 0x17 or 0x18 failures
Smart card or certificate configuration issues generating 0x10 failures
Time synchronization issues between clients and DCs generating 0x25 failures

keyFields.name: TargetUserName
keyFields.description: Account that failed pre-authentication; pivot for spray (many accounts) vs. brute force (one account)

keyFields.name: FailureCode
keyFields.description: 0x18 = bad password (most common); 0x10 = pre-auth not required; 0x25 = clock skew

keyFields.name: IpAddress
keyFields.description: Source IP of the AS-REQ; single IP hitting many accounts = password spray

keyFields.name: PreAuthType
keyFields.description: Pre-authentication method used; type 0 indicates no pre-auth required (AS-REP roasting target)

detectionRules.title: Kerberos Password Spray Detection
detectionRules.rule:
SecurityEvent
| where EventID == 4771
| where FailureCode == "0x18"
| summarize UniqueAccounts = dcount(TargetUserName), FailureCount = count() by IpAddress, bin(TimeGenerated, 5m)
| where UniqueAccounts > 10
detectionRules.notes: Single IP failing pre-auth against many accounts within 5 minutes. Tune UniqueAccounts threshold per environment size.

detectionRules.title: Kerberos Pre-Authentication Spray
detectionRules.rule:
index=wineventlog EventCode=4771 Failure_Code=0x18
| bin _time span=5m
| stats dc(Target_Account_Name) as UniqueAccounts count by _time, Client_Address
| where UniqueAccounts > 10
detectionRules.notes: Detects password spray via Kerberos pre-authentication failures from a single source.

detectionRules.title: Kerberos Pre-Authentication Failure — Spray Pattern
detectionRules.rule:
title: Kerberos Pre-Authentication Failure - Spray Pattern
status: experimental
logsource:
    product: windows
    service: security
detection:
    selection:
        EventID: 4771
        FailureCode: '0x18'
condition: selection | count() by IpAddress > 10
timeframe: 5m
falsepositives:
    - Misconfigured applications
    - User password change propagation delay
level: medium
detectionRules.notes: Threshold-based detection; may require SIEM-specific syntax adjustment.

entries.title: A Kerberos service ticket request failed
entries.summary: A Kerberos TGS service ticket request failed at the KDC.
entries.details: DC-only event generated when a TGS request fails — the client submitted a valid TGT but the service ticket request itself was rejected by the KDC. Key fields: ServiceName (the SPN that was requested), IpAddress (the requesting client), FailureCode. Common failure codes: 0x7 (server not found — the SPN does not exist, the primary indicator for SPN enumeration and scanning), 0x3d (inappropriate field in message), 0x32 (KDC cannot accommodate requested option). FailureCode 0x7 at volume from one source is the principal SPN enumeration indicator: tools such as Rubeus, Impacket, and PowerView enumerate all SPNs by requesting TGS tickets for each one, generating a burst of 4773 events with 0x7 for non-existent SPNs. Legitimate users rarely encounter 0x7 failures organically. This event is a useful precursor signal for Kerberoasting — SPN enumeration typically precedes targeted TGS requests against discovered service accounts.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Steal or Forge Kerberos Tickets: Kerberoasting
Account Discovery: Domain Account

investigationPivots: Multiple 4773 events with FailureCode 0x7 from the same IpAddress in rapid succession indicates automated SPN enumeration (precursor to Kerberoasting)
ServiceName values that follow sequential or alphabetical patterns suggest scripted enumeration rather than organic user activity
IpAddress correlating with recent BloodHound/SharpHound LDAP queries (EID 1644 on DCs) indicates a full AD reconnaissance session in progress
Single 4773 with 0x7 for a high-privilege SPN (krbtgt, administrator) from an unexpected host warrants investigation
commonFalsePositives: Applications with misconfigured or stale SPN references failing to authenticate to their target services
Service accounts referencing decommissioned services that no longer have registered SPNs

keyFields.name: ServiceName
keyFields.description: Target service for the failed TGS; repeated failures across many SPNs may indicate enumeration

keyFields.name: Status
keyFields.description: Failure code; 0x7 = server not found (SPN enumeration), 0x34 = ticket expired

keyFields.name: IpAddress
keyFields.description: Source IP of the failed request; pivot for SPN scanning

entries.title: A Kerberos TGT was denied because the device does not meet access control restrictions
entries.summary: A Kerberos TGT request was denied because the device failed an authentication policy or Protected Users restriction.
entries.details: DC-only event generated when a Kerberos Authentication Service (AS) request is denied because the requesting device fails access control restrictions imposed by an Authentication Policy or Authentication Policy Silo. These policies, introduced in Windows Server 2012 R2, allow organisations to restrict which devices are authorised to obtain TGTs for specific high-value accounts — for example, requiring that an account can only authenticate from Privileged Access Workstations (PAWs). Key fields: TargetUserName (the account that was denied), TargetDomainName, IpAddress (the requesting device). A 4820 event indicates that the correct credentials were supplied but the device is not permitted to use them under the active authentication policy — this is expected behaviour when a compromised credential is used from an untrusted device. Correlate with EID 4820 volume from a specific IpAddress to detect credential theft attempts using a valid password from an attacker-controlled machine. Contrast: EID 4771 fires for pre-auth failures (wrong password); EID 4820 fires for correct password but policy-restricted device.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Use Alternate Authentication Material: Pass the Ticket
Steal or Forge Kerberos Tickets: Golden Ticket

prerequisites.description: Authentication Policies or Authentication Policy Silos must be configured in Active Directory. Available on Windows Server 2012 R2 DCs and above. Accounts must be placed in the Protected Users security group or assigned to a policy.

investigationPivots: IpAddress not matching any known PAW or authorised admin workstation for the TargetUserName indicates a credential theft attempt from an untrusted device
Repeated 4820 for the same TargetUserName from multiple different IpAddresses indicates an attacker with a stolen credential attempting to authenticate from different hosts
Contrast with Security EID 4624 logon history for the TargetUserName — if the account normally logs on from a specific set of machines, any other source is anomalous
4820 events for Tier-0 accounts (Domain Admins, KRBTGT, Enterprise Admins) are highest severity
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate admin attempting to use a Tier-0 account from a non-PAW workstation (policy misconfiguration or user error)
Service accounts with authentication policies being accessed from a new server that has not been registered in the policy
Recently added machines not yet included in the Authentication Policy Silo

keyFields.name: TargetUserName
keyFields.description: Account denied the TGT; Tier-0 accounts are highest priority

keyFields.name: IpAddress
keyFields.description: Source device IP; must match an authorised device per the authentication policy

entries.title: A Kerberos service ticket was denied because the user, device, or both do not meet access control restrictions
entries.summary: A Kerberos TGS service ticket request was denied due to authentication policy or device restrictions.
entries.details: DC-only event generated when a TGS service ticket request is denied because the requesting user, device, or both fail the access control restrictions imposed by an Authentication Policy or Policy Silo. This is the TGS-phase equivalent of EID 4820 (which fires at the AS/TGT phase). Key fields: TargetUserName (the user requesting the service ticket), ServiceName (the target service SPN), IpAddress. This event is particularly valuable because it can fire even when the account has a valid TGT — the user authenticated successfully at AS but the service being accessed has additional device-level restrictions. Authentication Policy failures on high-value services (e.g., backup agents accessing domain controllers, SQL service accounts, Exchange services) are the primary investigation pivot. In a credential theft scenario, 4821 for a privileged service ticket indicates an attacker with a stolen TGT attempting to access restricted services from an unauthorised device.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Use Alternate Authentication Material: Pass the Ticket

prerequisites.description: Authentication Policies or Authentication Policy Silos must be configured in Active Directory. Available on Windows Server 2012 R2 DCs and above.

investigationPivots: ServiceName for a sensitive service (krbtgt, DCs, backup systems) combined with an unexpected IpAddress is highest priority
4821 after a successful 4768 (TGT issued) confirms the attacker has a valid TGT but cannot reach the target service — policy is working as designed; investigate the source IP
Cluster of 4821 events for the same TargetUserName across multiple ServiceName values indicates an attacker enumerating accessible services with a stolen credential
commonFalsePositives: Service accounts with restrictive authentication policies connecting from newly provisioned servers
Backup or monitoring tools accessing services from hosts not yet added to the authentication policy silo

keyFields.name: TargetUserName
keyFields.description: User denied the service ticket

keyFields.name: ServiceName
keyFields.description: Target service SPN that was denied; sensitive SPNs are highest priority

keyFields.name: IpAddress
keyFields.description: Requesting device; must be authorised per the authentication policy for this service

entries.title: NTLM authentication failed because the account is a member of the Protected Users group
entries.summary: An NTLM authentication attempt was denied because the account belongs to the Protected Users security group.
entries.details: DC-only event generated when an NTLM authentication attempt is rejected because the authenticating account is a member of the Protected Users global security group. The Protected Users group, introduced in Windows Server 2012 R2, enforces several security restrictions including: prohibiting NTLM authentication, prohibiting DES and RC4 Kerberos encryption, enforcing short TGT lifetimes, and preventing credential delegation. When an NTLM authentication is attempted for a Protected Users member — whether by a legacy application, an attacker using pass-the-hash, or an NTLM relay attack — the DC generates this event and denies the authentication. Key fields: TargetUserName, TargetDomainName, IpAddress. This event is a strong security indicator: it means an NTLM authentication was attempted for an account that should never use NTLM. NTLM relay and pass-the-hash attacks against Protected Users members will consistently generate 4822 and will never succeed — making this event a reliable indicator of attempted credential attack against a hardened account.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Use Alternate Authentication Material: Pass the Hash
Adversary-in-the-Middle: LLMNR/NBT-NS Poisoning and SMB Relay

prerequisites.description: The account must be a member of the Protected Users global security group. Domain controllers must be Windows Server 2012 R2 or later.

investigationPivots: Any 4822 event is anomalous — accounts in Protected Users should never be presented with NTLM challenges in a correctly configured environment
IpAddress of the NTLM authentication source is the primary investigation pivot — this is the attacker's relay host or the host performing pass-the-hash
TargetUserName for a Domain Admin or Tier-0 account is highest severity — indicates targeted credential attack against a privileged account
Correlate IpAddress with Responder/LLMNR poisoning indicators in DNS and DHCP logs to identify relay attack infrastructure
commonFalsePositives: Legacy applications that only support NTLM and are authenticating as a Protected Users member
Misconfigured services using Protected Users accounts against NTLM-only endpoints
Migration period where an account was recently added to Protected Users but service dependencies were not updated

keyFields.name: TargetUserName
keyFields.description: Protected Users member whose NTLM auth was blocked; Tier-0 accounts are highest priority

keyFields.name: IpAddress
keyFields.description: Source of the NTLM authentication attempt; this is the attacker or relay host

detectionRules.title: NTLM Auth Blocked for Protected Users Member
detectionRules.rule:
SecurityEvent
| where EventID == 4822
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, TargetUserName, IpAddress
| order by TimeGenerated desc
detectionRules.notes: Every 4822 event should be investigated. There is no valid reason for NTLM auth to be attempted for a Protected Users member in a well-configured environment.

detectionRules.title: NTLM Authentication Blocked for Protected Users Member
detectionRules.rule:
title: NTLM Authentication Blocked for Protected Users Member
status: stable
logsource:
    product: windows
    service: security
detection:
    selection:
        EventID: 4822
    condition: selection
falsepositives:
    - Legacy applications using Protected Users accounts with NTLM
    - Recent Protected Users group membership change not propagated to all services
level: high

entries.title: NTLM authentication failed because access control restrictions are required
entries.summary: An NTLM authentication was denied because the Authentication Policy requires Kerberos but NTLM was presented.
entries.details: DC-only event generated when an NTLM authentication attempt is denied because an Authentication Policy applied to the account requires that only Kerberos authentication be used. This differs from EID 4822 in that the restriction comes from an explicitly configured Authentication Policy (which can also restrict device access) rather than from Protected Users group membership. Key fields: TargetUserName, TargetDomainName, IpAddress, WorkstationName. Authentication Policies provide fine-grained control over which authentication protocols are permitted for specific accounts or silos. When an attacker attempts pass-the-hash or NTLM relay against an account covered by a strict Kerberos-only policy, they will receive this denial and EID 4823 will be generated on the DC. The combination of 4822 (Protected Users block) and 4823 (Policy block) together in proximity from the same source indicates an attacker systematically attempting NTLM authentication against hardened accounts — a red team or adversary reconnaissance pattern.

mitreAttack.techniqueName: Use Alternate Authentication Material: Pass the Hash
Adversary-in-the-Middle: LLMNR/NBT-NS Poisoning and SMB Relay

prerequisites.description: An Authentication Policy with NTLM restriction must be configured in Active Directory and applied to the account. Requires Windows Server 2012 R2 or later DCs.

investigationPivots: Any 4823 event warrants review — it means NTLM was attempted against an account with a Kerberos-only policy
WorkstationName or IpAddress identifies the source of the NTLM attempt; non-domain-joined machines or known-bad IPs are critical
4823 events alongside 4822 events from the same IpAddress indicate systematic NTLM-based credential attack against hardened accounts
Correlate IpAddress with Sysmon network connection events to identify the responsible process on the source host
commonFalsePositives: Applications enforced to use NTLM (e.g., older IIS apps with Windows Integrated Auth) connecting as policy-restricted accounts
Authentication policy applied to an account before legacy application dependencies were migrated to Kerberos
Monitoring or backup agents using Authentication Policy-protected service accounts

keyFields.name: TargetUserName
keyFields.description: Account denied NTLM auth by policy; identifies the targeted account in an attack

keyFields.name: IpAddress
keyFields.description: Source of the NTLM attempt; primary pivot for identifying the attacker host

keyFields.name: WorkstationName
keyFields.description: NetBIOS name of the source machine; corroborates IpAddress for source attribution
